commentaries.author: George Leo Haydock
commentaries.content: In the Greek it is called epiousion; i.e. super substantial. This is not the bread that goeth into the body, but the bread of eternal life, that supports the life of the soul. It is here called daily bread. Receive then daily, what will daily profit you; and continue so to live, that you may be daily in proper dispositions for receiving it. All who are under sin, have received a wound, and must seek for a cure. The cure is this heavenly and most venerable sacrament. (St. Augustine, Serm. ii. de verbo Dei.)

commentaries.author: Tertullian of Carthage
commentaries.content:
Whose kingdom shall I wish to come-his, of whom I never heard as the king of glory; or His, in whose hand are even the hearts of kings? Who shall give me my daily.
In our ordinary prayer likewise commanding us to request "bread"

commentaries.author: The Didache
commentaries.content: Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done, as in heaven, so on earth. Give us to-day our daily (needful) bread,
